displayTitle: 2025-12-15 - Māori Affairs Committee - Ngāti Rāhiri Tumutumu Claims Settlement Bill
topic: Ngāti Rāhiri Tumutumu Claims Settlement Bill
description: Monday 15 December 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee Ngāti Rāhiri Tumutumu Claims Settlement Bill Ngati Tumutumu Trust Daniel Braid, Chairperson June Mccaskill Frank Pakura Tui Pa Landowners Nicole Pakura Ike Williams Tui Pa Marae Committee Thelma Barbarich June McCaskill Mark Fitzgibbon Nicole Pakura Jill Taylor Nicki Scott, Co-negotiatior and Lawyer/Director for Bush Forbes Lawyers Ngati Tamatera Treaty of Waitangi Negotiators John McEnteer
descriptionSummary: Ngāti Rāhiri Tumutumu Claims Settlement Bill. Ngati Tumutumu Trust. Daniel Braid, Chairperson

chunks.text: Well, um, it's a pleasure to be here. Um, I have the role of being the chair of the, the committee, uh, today. I stand here before you today with deep humility, carrying the voices of my parents, my cousins, my aunties and uncles, and all those who have shaped my journey that brings us growing up at the par is my wife. Growing up here at the par meant different for each of us.

For me, being a marae baby was not just a place, but about the people. Before the Mara was erected beside our home, there were homesteads, two homesteads of two sisters who created that path, who helped us navigate what we were supposed to do as those houses where a place where cousins, nannies and aunties and uncles supported one another.

It was there where we learned kaka built sports teams, celebrated our joys and mourned our losses. The invisible fence line between two sisters that never a barrier because of the strength of the wau was greater than any boundaries. This was a found foundation of our why and my why, but this is then my why.

Too often those two call themselves whanau, who sit around tables speaking only to each other about trials and tribulations, the wins, the glories, and the moving forward wins and losses are shared amongst only themselves or within those circles. Yet we as whanau, hapu iwi should be privy to those journeys, the victories and the setbacks alike.

Because what affects us affects us all. My why is to break open those circles to ensure that struggles and triumphs are all shared within us. Our papa struggles. In the past two years, some of our final have been forced. The final alternative accommodation members that brought Wau papa, not only because they wanted to, but because they could not to have a wau, could not afford to carry the debt left behind.

This is not a financial burden, it was a spiritual and emotional one. There were Wau who had always to talk with the whose hands and hearts where, where wherever help was needed was there. And yet they too were made to struggle, to sacrifice, to carry the weight of decisions that have been shared more openly with us all.

Yet, the land sits empty. No home, no han on for my sister sacrifice, my sister, who, me, our cousins, and even the iwi trust. When we returned, she was the one who called, who led us to Kaia, who set up and gathered Kai, who built memories for us all.

And yet she is suffering the most her. That was shown. She was, it was all in vain. She was left to find emergency housing again while members were able to purchase homesteads, that at the time the Iwi trust houses were available. But in no way, shape, or form did they offer to help.
Emergency housing for our wau is not acceptable. When the iwi could have provided service to help, that was not the Copa we meant to stand for.

Who are these people and who are they supposed to be navigating? Are they supposed to navigate our iwi into something greater when the message they send is so clear? Messages of exclusion, pressure of disregard for the very whanau that hold this place together. We are still struggling with so much in this town, and yet the things that are offered do not truly benefit the whanau who desperately need them.

A marai has been called the bane of existence, labeled as to toxic by the same people who now host the Hui. Right here at this place of significance, is it that contradiction cuts deep because us the marai is nott toxic. It's the heartbeat of our whanau, the place where our stories live, where our tears have fallen.

And where our laughter has echoed to di dismiss it is to dismiss the generations we have built and carried it who have kept us alive. And yet, despite those words, the rai still stance. It still welcomes, it still holds us together. That is the strength of our people to enjoy criticism, enjoy hardship, and to continue to rise between the Mara.

Because the Mara is more than just the building. It's us rekindling the fire of why I'm here. Why I moved back. My cousin had reached out with an idea and it was a beautiful idea, and it still is to light the fires that were desperately needed for our wwi.

Our, it was a dream to create mahi to strengthen the collective purpose and to remind us of who we actually are. I moved here with my whanau and to put those cord into action, to together, we built nurseries, extending knowledge to whanau who became ka mahi in a fund project. This was not about plants or buildings, it was about planting a seed hope, resilience, and the identity, or the Mona has always stood as a guardian watching over us.

The restoration eradication work we undertook was seen by some as impossible. But we know as our tu knew that nothing is impossible. When we au stand together for the Mona by the Mona, we prove that challenges can overcome. Every step we took was a reminder that our strength lies not in individuals, but a collective heartbeat of iwi and a he body.

And yet, as we stand here today, there has been no open a GM, no hui publicly or courted or shared on, on social media. Decisions have been made behind closed doors. And I cannot stand by quietly. I'm opposing that the settlement is currently as it stands because before any settlement comes through, we as an iwi, hapu, and whanau should be ready, already be flourishing.

Before this money even came through, we should already see the fruits of our mahi, the strength of our unity and transparency of our leadership. I should not be standing here opposing to this happening, but I am and I do because my wife is clear. Our whanau, our hapu, our iwi, and our deserve better.

We deserve honesty, inclusion in the future, built together, not a part. And you, I just, Yeah. And that's it. And at the end of it, I am, I stand here today of the y this settlement of our y the trust of the whanau. Our journey is not about history, but about the future.
We are building for our, we are here because our ancestors dreamed of us to be here because our w fought for us. And we are here because we choose to carry on the fire for
